data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Expedia Gains On Uber Takeover Report
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Expedia (EXPE) gains after report Uber (UBER) explored potential takeover. (00:24) Meta Platforms (META) laying off employees at WhatsApp, Instagram. (01:28) Be our guest....for a hefty price: Disney (DIS) offering Lightning Lane Premier Pass. (02:19)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE) is up 7% in premarket action after a report that Uber (NYSE:UBER) considered a possible takeover of the company as it tries to diversify its business.

Uber (UBER) talked to advisers in recent months about a bid for Expedia (EXPE) after an acquisition of the company was broached by a third party.

This information is from a Financial Times report late Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

Uber's (UBER) interest was described as being at a very early stage and no deal may come to fruition, according to the report. No formal approach has been made by the ride-sharing company, and there are no current talks.

The focus of Uber's discussions was the role of Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi, who was Expedia’s CEO from 2005 to 2017 and remains a non-executive director, the FT said. Any approach for Expedia would likely be friendly given the CEO's relationship, and he would need to recuse himself from deal talks.

Uber, Expedia and Expedia Chairman Barry Diller declined to comment to the FT.

Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has initiated layoffs across various divisions, specifically targeting teams within WhatsApp, Instagram, and Reality Labs.

Although the exact number of job cuts has not been disclosed, reports indicate that they are relatively small in scale.

“Today, a few teams at Meta are making changes to ensure resources are aligned with their long-term strategic goals and location strategy,” company spokesperson Dave Arnold told The Verge. “This includes moving some teams to different locations, and moving some employees to different roles. In situations like this when a role is eliminated, we work hard to find other opportunities for impacted employees,” he added.

Notably, these layoffs follow a broader trend of workforce reductions at Meta (META), which has seen ~21,000 jobs cut since late 2022 and are a part of the broader cost-cutting initiatives, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg labeling 2023 as the "year of efficiency."

Meta (META) is set to release its Q3 earning results on Wednesday, October 30, after market close.

If you plan on visiting Disney World or Disneyland anytime soon, there’s a chance you’ll spend even more than you originally planned.

Disney (NYSE:DIS) will now let you enjoy the happiest place on earth without waiting in line and without limits.

The company is launching its “Lightning Lane Premier Pass” at its Florida and California theme parks to a limited number of guests at its deluxe resorts. This is not to be confused with the current “Lightning Lane Pass.”

For an additional fee of $137 to $478 (on top of the cost of admission), visitors can enter Lightning Lane attractions without having to book in advance, as is the case with the current Lightning Lane Multi Pass and Single Pass.

Disney World guests can use the pass to enter each attraction once a day, and must be purchased separately for each individual park. Florida’s Disney World will begin the pilot program of the Lightning Lane Premier Pass on October 30 with limited availability, and it’ll be available at California’s Disneyland on October 23.

The extra fee for the Lightning Lane Premier Pass varies based on the theme park and the time of year with the most expensive price of $329-$449 at Disney’s Magic Kingdom and the least expensive price of $129 to $199 at Animal Kingdom.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

Oklo, NuScale Power hit all-time highs on Amazon's plans to go big on nuclear

BlackBerry shows plan to return to profitability next year at investor day

No more shrinkflation? PepsiCo says it will add more potato chips to some bags

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is up 0.3% at $70/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $67,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.7%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is up 7% following the release of better-than-expected Q3 results.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Initial Jobless Claims. The number of individuals filing for unemployment benefits in the U.S. is expected to decline to 241K from 258K, highest level in 14 months.

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales. September retail sales are expected to rise 0.3% over the prior month after gaining 0.1% in August.

  • 10:00 am Housing Market Index. The Housing Market Index is expected to rise for the second month to 43 after gaining to 41 in September.

data.title: Reiterated Strong Buy: Unit Corporation's Leaner Operations Still Look Bullish
data.description:
Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC) has focused on stabilizing its financials and continues to be centered on delivering reliable dividends for its investors. Naturally, the recent dividend cut wasn’t welcomed, but after analyzing the company, I still believe there’s a viable long-term bull case for UNTC. Management seems to be on track as of Q2 2024 despite fluctuating market conditions, reduced revenues, and lower production volumes. However, this was mostly due to the company's ongoing divestitures since 2022. On top of that, the contract drilling segment saw reduced demand and pricing rates, which compounded its challenges. Nevertheless, I think UNTC now has a leaner operation, and as long as revenues stabilize, its dividends seem much more sustainable. Thus, I feel it’s fair to reiterate my “Strong Buy” on UNTC for investors who understand its inherent turnaround risks.

Still an Income Play: Business Overview

Unit Corporation (UNTC) is an energy company with a diversified Oil and Gas portfolio. UNTC extracts oil and natural gas from its properties, acquires properties already in production, and provides contract drilling services to other firms in the sector. UNTC employs specialized rigs and crews to manage its services onshore. Since my February 2023 article, UNTC has remained focused on being an income investment. Even though unfortunately, it had to cut its dividend by half, and its revenues continued declining due to lower production levels.

More recently, UNTC’s oil and natural gas subsidiary reported an increase in average oil prices. Management noted that they saw a 38% rise in Q2 to $79.23 per barrel compared to $57.34 per barrel in the same period in 2023. However, these increments were not enough to compensate for its production volume decrease. You see, oil production dropped by a whopping 49%, and natural gas production fell by 43% YoY in Q2 2024.

Nevertheless, I think it’s worth highlighting that UNTC’s declining production volumes are mostly due to its strategic asset divestitures. These divestitures have been ongoing since 2022, and in 2023, the company shed even more non-core assets. Those core assets were valued at approximately $107 million. UNTC then used those proceeds to bolster its cash reserves and double down on its strategy of focusing on core investments and reducing liabilities. Another positive from this downsizing was that plugging and abandonment costs associated with decommissioning old wells also decreased.

Properties and Financials: Sustainable Dividend Strategy

Furthermore, UNTC’s contract drilling subsidiary reduced its contract pricing and had a lower demand for these services. Despite maintaining the number of available rigs to 14, the average use rate decreased from 15.6 rigs in Q2 2023 to 11.9 rigs in use in Q2 2024. This reduction could be due to competition or external market conditions. Nevertheless, the key factor that affected UNTC’s revenues was the average rig price rates, which declined by 3%, from $31,764 to $30,786.

Currently, UNTC’s main operating area is the Permian Basin in Texas, one of the US’s most oil-productive regions. The company also operates in Oklahoma, including the Anadarko Basin, where its oil and natural gas wells are located. Additionally, UNTC’s Louisiana infrastructure exposes it to another rich oil and gas region. New Mexico is also part of the Permian Basin. Overall, I think UNTC’s remaining assets are located in areas with high production potential that align with the company’s strategy to concentrate on core, abundant regions.

Simultaneously, UNTC decided to keep its quarterly dividend payments at $1.25 per share in Q2 2024. This is key because most of its investor base is undoubtedly looking for a consistent dividend regardless of fluctuating income. The company paid $12.9 million in dividends this quarter, funded by available cash. Moreover, on September 16, the company announced that the quarterly cash dividend for Q3 2024 would also be $1.25 per share.

So, this dividend seems to be the new normal payout going forward. But more importantly, it does corroborate that management remains committed to consistently returning value to its shareholders through regular cash dividends. Additionally, I believe the company seems financially stable and debt-free, so the recent pullback might be a good opportunity for long-term investors.

Relatively Cheap: Valuation Analysis

You see, from a valuation perspective, UNTC now trades at a $318.3 million market cap, making it essentially a microcap now. Its balance sheet holds $80.8 million in cash and equivalents against no long-term debt. However, the company does have about $55.6 million of regular liabilities and a book value of $257.1 million as of Q2 2024. This also indicates a P/B of 1.2. For comparison, its sector’s median P/B is 1.4, so UNTC seems slightly undervalued.

Additionally, over the trailing twelve-month [TTM] period, I estimate that UNTC has generated a positive cash flow of $79.3 million by adding its CFOs and CAPEX. This calculation excludes the $61.2 million it received by selling its PPE over that period, so it’s actually a relatively conservative assessment. Still, I do think it’s worth mentioning that back in Q1 2023, UNTC had accumulated depreciation worth 35.7% of its gross PPE. However, as of Q2 2024, it’s now 45.6% of gross PPE, which suggests that UNTC is investing less in maintenance CAPEX, which might have acted as a cash flow tailwind. Alternatively, it’s also possible that UNTC has sold its less depreciated assets.

Furthermore, UNTC generated $12.1 million in operating income in Q2 2024, which annualized implies a $48.4 million run rate. This would price UNTC today at 6.6 times its operating income, which does seem relatively undervalued. Moreover, I think its high dividend yield seems safe for the foreseeable future as long as revenues stabilize. Therefore, I still lean bullish on UNTC, rating it a “Strong Buy” for long-term income investors who are willing to accept UNTC’s inherent turnaround risks.

Investment Caveats: Risk Analysis

However, the main drawback with UNTC is that its revenues are declining significantly. Since I last covered it back in February 2023, its quarterly revenues have decreased from $93.9 million in Q1 2023 to just $56.8 million in Q2 2024. This is a notable 39.7% decrease, which could very well persist going forward. Moreover, UNTC also cut its quarterly dividend from what used to be $2.50 per share to just $1.25 per share today. Yet, since its stock price is currently around $32.50 today, that’s still a respectable 15.4% annualized dividend yield. The only issue is that this compelling dividend yield holds some risks as long as UNTC’s revenues continue deteriorating.

Furthermore, in their Q1 2024 investor presentation, management mentioned that they were also reducing G&A expenses to reflect their smaller footprint. Thus, for all intents and purposes, UNTC is now adjusting to a much more downsized reality. The only question remains whether or not they’ll continue downsizing if revenues trend even lower or if they’ll stabilize and set a new potential base for future growth around these levels.

Yet, last time I covered UNTC, their SG&A expenses reached $5.1 million in Q1 2023, but as of Q2 2024, they’re now slightly higher at $5.6 million. It’s not a huge increase, but it also raises some questions about their actual downsizing of expenses. Likewise, their gross margins have deteriorated from 53.1% in Q1 2023 to 37.4% in Q2 2024. The same applies to EBIT margins, which declined from 58.0% to 21.3% over the same period.

So, I’m skeptical about management actually reducing expenses to reflect their new, downsized reality. Naturally, it could be explained away by arguing that revenues have declined much faster than expected, outpacing UNTC’s cost-cutting efforts. Thus, as long as revenues stabilize, their cost-cutting strategy will start to reflect improved margins. But this remains speculative until we see that reflected in their actual financials.

Fortunately, the main positive I found about their latest Q2 2024 figures was that UNTC’s latest quarterly cash flow was roughly $18.0 million by adding its CFOs and CAPEX, excluding what it received by selling $3.2 million worth of PPE. Also, it spent $1.1 million on stock repurchases and $12.9 million on paying dividends. Thus, in total, it needs roughly $14.0 million per quarter to sustain this level of stock repurchases and dividend payments. Since it’s already generating $18.0 million in positive cash flow, I think UNTC’s current 15.4% dividend yield seems sustainable. However, this is still contingent on revenues stabilizing.

Still a “Strong Buy”: Conclusion

Overall, UNTC has indeed faced headwinds since I last covered it last year. The main issue is its rapidly declining revenues, which can be explained to some extent due to its downsizing. However, going forward, the main variable to monitor is whether or not revenues stabilize and margins start improving. Fortunately, I think that its current cash flow run rate does suggest its dividend is sustainable at this point. Moreover, the dividend itself somewhat de-risks UNTC as well. So, in total, I think it’s still fair to lean bullish on UNTC, especially now that it’s a more focused operation with an already positive cash flow. Hence, I reiterate my “Strong Buy” rating for income investors who understand its embedded turnaround risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Schrödinger's Path Forward: AI, Cash Burn, And Clinical Uncertainty
data.description:
My last analysis on Schrödinger (NASDAQ:SDGR) was in January 2023. Given its strong revenue growth and promising AI-powered drug discovery platform, my recommendation was to "hold." However, I was concerned about the stock's high valuation and the uncertainty surrounding clinical trial success and profitability. Schrödinger's stock has fallen 64% since then, while the S&P 500 has returned 26%. So it seems like a good time to revisit my analysis to see if Schrödinger's poor stock performance is the result of fundamental changes or simply random chance.

Earlier this year, Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) elected to return the rights of an SOS1 inhibitor back to Schrödinger. The drug had been in IND-enabling stages, but Bristol had gained another SOS1 inhibitor after purchasing Mirati. So the Bristol collaboration, which is focused on a neurology asset, is still ongoing, and Schrödinger is still entitled to receive up to $482 million in development and market milestones (or "biobucks").

In May, Schrödinger's stock struggled after the company released Q1 earnings, which revealed misses on revenue and EPS, with a widening net loss. However, this should not come as a surprise to investors, as Schrödinger's revenues, for now, are entirely dependent on collaborations and clinical trial developments, which are inherently scattered and difficult to predict. Schrödinger's guidance for 2024 remained the same. They projected drug discovery revenue to be between $30 million and $35 million and software revenue growth to be from 6% to 13%.

For Q2, total revenue was $47.3 million, compared to $35.2 million in the second quarter of '23. This was mostly attributed to increases in software revenue, which reached $35.4 million in Q2. The net loss increased to $54 million.

Schrödinger revised its 2024 financial outlook to account for lower gross margins in the software segment. They balanced this by a minor adjustment to their "operating expense growth" forecast from 8-12% to 8-10%.

Schrödinger is also advancing its platform to include toxicity prediction. This, of course, is all aimed at lowering the drug development costs (e.g., avoiding clinical failures due to toxicity).

The company's proprietary pipeline, which includes SGR-1505, is in Phase 1 development. 1505 is a MALT1 (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma translocation protein 1) inhibitor being tested for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. MALT1 is a signaling pathway for cancer cell survival that "drives tumor cell survival, proliferation, and metastasis." 1505 is designed to overcome past safety issues (via higher potency and specificity) of earlier MALT1 inhibitors such as immune suppression, cytokine release syndrome, and hematological toxicity. There are several treatments available for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies (CAR-T cell therapies, bispecific antibodies, BTK inhibitors, and so on), so SGR-1505 will need to deliver very convincing safety and efficacy data to have any chance at market success. Initial clinical data in the Phase 1 dose-escalation study is expected in 1H 2025.

Financial Health

As of June 30, Schrödinger had $108.109 million in cash and cash equivalents. Marketable securities totaled $269.18 million. Total current assets were $449.179 million, while total current liabilities were $99.759 million. This implies a current ratio well over 2, which indicates that Schrödinger can readily cover its short-term obligations. Schrödinger is without significant long-term debts beyond its lease liabilities.

In the first six months of 2024, Schrödinger utilized $92.999 million in operating activities. This implies a monthly cash burn of $15.4 million. If we divide their cash and marketable securities by this figure, it suggests a cash runway of approximately 2 years.

Note: Cash runway and burn estimates are based on historical data and my analysis and should be viewed as estimates only. The term "cash burn" can encompass various factors beyond cash flow, making these estimates somewhat subjective. If companies provide forward-looking estimates in their earnings releases, I will include them to supplement my analysis.

SDGR Stock: Risk/Reward Analysis and Investment Recommendation

The hype surrounding AI-related drug developers like Schrödinger that peaked following NVIDIA (NVDA) investments has waned and the reality of clinical failure has set in. For example, in September, Recursion (RXRX) plunged after disappointing Phase 2 data for its lead asset, REC-994, procured underwhelming data in cerebral cavernous malformation, a rare condition. Although, as I pointed out months before CCM data, 994's disappointing showing was not a surprise. Although AI may have had some role in 994's preclinical development, it was hardly a novel idea, as 994 is a repurposed old compound (tempol). So, I do not believe this was a fair "proof-of-concept" for AI-powered drugs. So far, AI's role in medicine has been to improve success rates in Phase 1 trials, and there is significant value in this. A recent peer-reviewed article described how AI-derived drugs "could have a success rate of 90%-90% above the historical average" because of "algorithmic effects and validated biological targets that reduce the toxicity risk." In this context, Schrödinger's prospects look particularly promising, but it doesn't come without significant operational and financial risks.

Operationally, there are several companies vying for space in AI-enabled drug discovery, even including Google (GOOG). Recursion's setback in Phase 2 demonstrates that clinical development, regulatory approval, and market success are not guaranteed. Schrödinger's own pipeline may experience setbacks of its own. Financially, Schrödinger faces significant net losses as well as high R&D investments required to remain relevant (but possibly insufficient to remain competitive against infinitely larger companies like Google).

In conclusion, SDGR may work as a Quadrant 1 investment in a barbell portfolio, but although Schrödinger's valuation is easier to stomach, there is too much uncertainty to merit a buy rating (maintain "hold").

data.title: Canada Goose: Winter Is Coming, And It's Not Going To Be A Good One (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The stock market doesn’t seem to think that we’re going to enter a recession after a parade of bank earnings showed healthy results. However, not all sectors seem to agree: in particular, the consumer space and luxury goods seem to be continuing to suffer from a pullback in consumer budgets. LVMH, one of the world’s largest portfolios of luxury brands, crashed after reporting weak earnings, blaming a slowdown in Chinese consumption.

Against this backdrop, Canada Goose (NYSE:GOOS) looks incredibly risky as well. The maker of thousand-dollar parka jackets is gearing up for its big winter season, but even in the less-important summer months, negative trends have formed. Yet in spite of brewing challenges, Canada Goose stock has zigzagged between gains and losses this year, settling at down single digits YTD. In my view, there’s a lot more downside left to go.

I last wrote a neutral hold opinion on Canada Goose in June, when the stock was still trading at $13 per share. Since then, the company has released very negative Q2 results, while much more recently, read-through from fellow luxury players like LVMH suggest that a bruising quarter is ahead for Canada Goose - which is quite untimely as the company is expecting to generate three-quarters of its revenue in its fiscal second half of the year, or September through March. With this in mind, I’m downgrading Canada Goose to a sell.

There are three main risks that are clouding my outlook on Canada Goose:

  • Macro malaise and a pullback in luxury spending. Despite an S&P 500 near all-time highs suggesting that we’ve averted the worst from a macro perspective, the same may not be true of all sectors, and consumers are still showing signs of trading down to less-expensive goods to preserve their budgets.
  • Severe decline in wholesale sales. Canada Goose itself is noting that its wholesalers are also tightening their budgets. While Canada Goose has been busy building up its store network and its online presence to boost DTC sales, the company relies on wholesalers to put its brand in front of more customers. Ultimately, fewer wholesalers stocking Canada Goose products means much less brand exposure.
  • China weakness. China results were a bright spot for Canada Goose in Q4, but it seems to have been a temporary blip, with China growth decelerating sharply again. And to hope on any fiscal stimulus from Beijing to support Chinese purchases on foreign luxury goods is probably too optimistic.

Steer clear here: in my view, Canada Goose has a lot more left to lose.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Canada Goose’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q1 (June quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Revenue grew only 4% y/y to CAD $88.1 million. We’ll now go through each of the key issues in turn. The first, as shown in the chart below, is the massive decline in wholesale orders. Wholesale revenue tanked -41% y/y (or -42% y/y on a constant currency basis) to just $16.0 million.

Now, a note here. The summer months are not hugely critical for Canada Goose, as the majority of its revenue still sits in winter jackets, despite its efforts to diversify its product lines into other categories like accessories and footwear. The bright side of the coin here may be that retailers and resellers may not want to stretch their working capital to order Canada Goose jackets as early as the June quarter, if they won’t display them until later in the winter.

However, the weaker sales that have now been ongoing for several quarters suggests that at least some portion of Canada Goose’s B2B buyer universe may be permanently destocking their wares - ultimately the smart choice if budget-conscious consumers aren’t buying its parkas anyway. Recall that Canada Goose’s outlook for FY25, which it affirmed in Q1, calls for wholesale revenue to decline -20% y/y for the full year.

The company notes that it aims to “improve the quality” of the partners that stock Canada Goose products, which may be a euphemism to make Canada Goose appear stronger when some partners are exiting of their own accord. Per CFO Neil Bowden’s remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

This decline reflected our planned lower order book, as we tightened supply to wholesale partners in a soft business environment and the continued optimization of wholesale relationships as we elevate the quality of our partners in this sales channel.

Overall, we're confident in delivery of our order book to wholesale partners for the full year and continue to expect wholesale revenue to decrease 20% year-over-year on a full year basis. We delivered some incremental product in Q1, albeit small, above and beyond the planned order book with select partners.”

Alongside weak channel results, we also note that China revenue slowed considerably. Growth in Q1 in the region was only 11% on a constant currency basis, as shown in the chart below: which compares very poorly against 32% constant currency growth in the preceding Q4. China is already a meaningful (one-quarter) chunk of Canada Goose’s sales, and its fastest growing region by far: so the slowdown here hurts tremendously.

Lastly, to add insult to injury: gross margins in the first quarter declined 540 bps y/y to 59.7%. Make no mistake: a ~60% margin is still quite meaningful for a consumer products company. We note as well that the company cited 330bps of this reduction was self-inflicted, due to supply chain diversification efforts from a recently acquired new factory in Europe, which is producing goods at a higher cost.

However, we note here that the much higher mix of DTC, full-price sales (as opposed to wholesale trade orders at a discounted rate) should be a big benefit to margins, but that impact is being buried. As a result, adjusted EBIT losses widened to CAD -$96 million.

Q2 preview

Canada Goose is expected to report fiscal Q2 (September quarter) results sometime in early November. Now, Q1 was not a great catalyst for the stock, as the aforementioned China slowdown and failure to raise the full-year FY25 outlook didn't do much to appease investor concerns. In my view, Q2 may add further concerns to the mix.

Key items to watch will be deceleration in China, as well as a potential reversion of the U.S. to a revenue decline (versus slight growth in Q1). It's not just Chinese consumers that are pulling back on demand, but amid a potentially more budget-conscious holiday shopping season, many U.S. retailers may be pulling back their order books even further. It's important to note that Canada Goose is expecting a ~20% wholesale decline this year in its outlook: but if Q2 doesn't improve substantially beyond the -42% constant-currency decline that it reported in Q1, investors will be scratching their heads on how Canada Goose will meet its forecast. Again, given the negative read-through from LVMH, it's more likely than not that Canada Goose's Q2 earnings print will be a downside driver for the stock.

Key takeaways

In my view, the clouds are continuing to darken for Canada Goose. In particular, I think the company is already lagging behind several key metrics it set out in its outlook:

The recently affirmed outlook calls for a -20% decline in wholesale revenue (versus a -40% decline in Q1), similar gross margins to FY24 (versus a five point decline in Q1) and 100bps of Adjusted EBIT expansion in FY24 (versus a two point decline in Q1). The bright side of this coin, of course, is to write off Q1 results since they’re the product of a summer quarter that doesn’t contribute much to Canada Goose’s full year results. But in my view, these trends, plus the more recent negative read-through from other luxury industry peers, points to a bleak year ahead for this company.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Rates Spark: Markets Positioned For Back-To-Back ECB Cuts
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker and Benjamin Schroeder

ECB cut most likely, but sharp market positioning prevents the front end going lower

We think the ECB will cut by 25bp, and so do markets, but holding the deposit rate at 3.5% is still possible, in our view. Not cutting would be a big surprise to rates given the implied probability of swaps is north of 90% and has been there for two weeks. After this meeting, markets price in consecutive cuts of 25bp for the next four to come, and thus, a disappointment now would challenge the entire trajectory going forward.

Also, if the ECB cuts as expected, we see a considerable chance that market expectations won’t be met in terms of communication. The ECB will likely want to keep flexibility about a December cut and will push back against the idea that this is the start of consecutive cuts, as is currently priced in. On the other hand, ECB President Christine Lagarde may place more emphasis on mounting growth concerns, which could be caught as a dovish tone by markets. Having said that, we deem it unlikely markets will move to pricing in a probability for 50bp cuts for the time being, and thus, the front-end of the curve should be supported from below.

A pushback against current pricing (or even a decision to hold rates) could see some flattening of the EUR swap curve. Not cutting would risk a broader risk-off move, as worries about a policy error would rise considerably. In this case, the terminal policy rate of the ECB could actually fall back below the neutral range of 2-2.25% as recession risks get priced in more. With little scope for the front end to go lower, we go into the meeting with a bias towards a flatter curve.

Thursday’s events and market view

A cut from the ECB is seen as a done deal, not just looking at market pricing. All 37 economists surveyed by Bloomberg see a cut. If delivered, the cut itself could be a non-event for markets, and the focus will mainly fall on communication with no new ECB forecasts to be presented this time.

US data should become more influential again. For retail sales, the strong auto sales should provide a good baseline, and our economists look for 0.4% MoM growth given decent consumer credit card borrowing numbers. Industrial production holds some downside after weak survey data. Markets tend to be more sensitive to the jobless claims, which nudged higher last week. Hurricanes and knock-on effects of strikes have led to some volatility, but this time a stable level is anticipated.

Primary markets will see Spain in the market reopening 7Y to 24Y bonds for up to €5.5bn. France will also be in the market selling shorter-dated bonds out to the 5y maturity and linkers, up to €14.5bn in total.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Fair Isaac Corporation: The Valuation Is Getting Ridiculous Now
data.description:
It seems as though Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO), the credit-scoring firm that I'll refer to as FICO, can't stop going up. In the past year, it has gained 126%, but now, the valuation is starting to get a bit ridiculous. Also, at the current valuation, buybacks, which are a part of the company's strategy, will be less effective and will affect per-share growth. This clouds the bull thesis even further.

Despite FICO being an amazing company with a competitive advantage, I'm staying out of it for now due to the high price tag, giving it a Hold rating. I wouldn't dare short it, though, because it's a strong company on a strong uptrend.

The Stock Price Keeps Outpacing Earnings Growth

In the last 10 years, FICO's price has risen by 2,730%. However, its normalized earnings per share have only increased by 601%. In the past couple of years or so, the gap has widened dramatically.

Essentially, most of its gains since the 2010s have come from a crazy amount of valuation multiple expansion, with the normalized P/E ratio and P/S ratio at all-time highs based on the chart below. Multiple expansion can't happen forever unless FICO suddenly finds a way to accelerate its growth rate in a meaningful way, which is not easy to do.

But Why Does FICO Stock Keep Rising?

Besides the recent rise in price due to what is likely irrational exuberance at this point, FICO is an excellent company with stable growth and a competitive advantage, so it makes sense that investors love it and keep bidding it higher. If you look at the first chart I posted above, you'll see how stable the EPS growth has been. On top of that, its free cash flow has grown at an impressive rate (and relatively steadily) over the past 10 years.

Investors Love FICO's Competitive Advantage

What impresses me about FICO is its ability to consistently become more profitable over the years. 10 years ago, its gross profit margin was 67.5%. Now, its TTM gross profit margin is a very high 79.35%. Below, you can see how its free cash flow and operating income margins grew as well (to quite high levels). FICO wouldn't be able to maintain its high growth rates and rising profit margins if competitors were hurting it.

The fact of the matter is that FICO is dominant in its industry. In fact, FICO scores are used in 90% of US mortgages. This brand dominance alone is a competitive advantage. Financial institutions are unlikely to stop using FICO scores because they work just fine and have been for many years.

The Current Valuation Will Hurt EPS Growth

FICO returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases, which boosts EPS growth. In the Q3 earnings call, FICO's CEO, Will Lansing, said the following:

We delivered record free cash flow of $206 million in our third quarter and $551 million over the last four quarters. We continue to return capital to our shareholders through buybacks. In Q3, we repurchased 196,000 shares at an average price of $1,293 per share, and we announced a new Board authorization for $1 billion of share repurchase.

In Q3, the average repurchase price was $1,293 per share. Is management still going to be repurchasing shares at over $2,000 per share? And if they do, they won't be able to buy as many shares with that $1 billion repurchase program, which will slow down EPS growth.

From Fiscal 2015 to 2023, FICO's buyback yield had consistently been over 2%. Sometimes, it would be near 5%, and it even reached over 10% in Fiscal 2022. However, the buyback yield for the trailing 12 months is 1.5%, and this number will likely stay depressed if the share price remains elevated.

It's funny how the price goes up as investors price in more growth, but the stock's rally itself will cause EPS growth to slow down. Sure, management can just decide to hold on to the cash and receive interest on it, but that also won't yield high returns relative to buybacks at a good price.

Analyzing The Valuation

I can already tell you that FICO stock is overpriced based on its 108x GAAP P/E ratio, but let's dive deeper. To value FICO, I'll use an H-model calculator that I created.

Explaining The H-Model And My Inputs

The H-model starts with a high-growth rate for FCF per share, which I set to 32%. This is the growth rate that will be seen in year 1. The reason I chose this growth rate is that from Fiscal 2024 to 2025, analysts expect 30.6% growth in FCF. I then brought it up to 32% to factor in a likely small share count reduction from buybacks. Then, the growth rate will fall in a linear fashion to the terminal growth rate of 3%.

I set the high-growth period to 20 years because FICO has been around since 1956 and is still growing quickly, so I doubt it'll suddenly reach "maturity" within 10 years. I'm also doing it to show that even with such a long period and high growth rate, the stock is still overpriced.

With the inputs above, the growth rate will fall by 1.45% every year until it reaches 3% in year 21. For example, that gives it a growth rate of 24.75% in year 5, 17.5% in year 10, and 10.25% in year 15.

I set 3% as the terminal growth rate (instead of 2% or 2.5%, which is closer to the US's expected GDP and inflation growth) because FICO's pricing power and competitive advantage can allow it to rise prices faster than inflation. Also, there will be the positive effect of buybacks.

Regarding FCF per share, I took the regular TTM FCF figure of $562.3 million and subtracted stock-based compensation ($143.6 million in the past 12 months) to factor in dilution and then used the basic share count of 24,518,533 to calculate it. This gives you FCF per share (adjusted for SBC) of around $17.077.

Lastly, I calculated 10.8% as the discount rate (cost of equity) using the CAPM model.

The result is a fair value of $860.42 per share, much lower than the last price of $2051.78. See below.

Perhaps you think 20 years is not enough time and that FICO will grow for much longer. That's fine. However, you'd need to increase the length of the high growth period to over 57 years to reach the stock's current price. Having to forecast growth this far into the future just doesn't give investors enough of a margin of safety, which is my problem with the valuation.

The Takeaway

FICO is an excellent company and stock. It clearly has a competitive advantage based on its financials and has grown steadily and swiftly in the past. It's also expected to keep growing in the future, with 30.6% FCF growth expected in Fiscal 2025 (as well as solid growth expected in both revenue and EPS).

However, the stock price has vastly outpaced fundamental growth recently, leaving the stock overvalued. My H-model valuation above has to price in many decades of high growth in order to achieve a fair value at the stock's current price. It's just too risky to project that far out. Lots of the stock's gains have come from valuation multiple expansion, which isn't sustainable.

Additionally, at the current valuation, buybacks will be less effective, hurting EPS growth and weakening the bull case slightly.

As a result, I view the stock as a Hold.

data.title: BWX Technologies: New Contract Awarded, But Timing Matters
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome CY Equity Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Recently, BWX Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:BWXT) was awarded the contract for High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium (HALEU) deconversion services by the U.S. Department of Energy (DoE). It's worth noting that BWXT is not the sole successful bidder - the contracts will be shared with other five companies (Centrus (LEU), Framatome, GE Vernova (GEV), Orano, and Westinghouse (CCJ)). And contracts are for up to 10 years, with a minimum of $2 million per company. The total amount available for these services is up to $800 million.

While this again confirms the close relationship between BWXT and the US government, I do not think this contract (and the revenue it will generate) will significantly improve the fundamentals of BWXT. Also, compared to a quarterly revenue of more than $600m, the increase from a new contract may be negligible.

My overall impression is that the market may have overvalued the stock. The current P/E (TTM) is ~41. This seems too high for a mature business, given that the firm's prospects haven't changed much over time. The stock price has gone up by more than 50% in the past year. At the same time, I did not see any headwinds that would significantly alter people's opinion. Therefore, for the shareholders and potential investors, I would suggest exercising some caution against the current valuation and suggest a Hold rating for BWXT.

How Do We Understand The Business?

BWX Technologies is a leading supplier of nuclear components and services, with a particular focus on the defense sector and nuclear power industry. The company's operations are highly predictable due to long-term government contracts and a steady demand for its specialized services. The core business (government operations) depends on the procurement needs of the US government. If you look at the company's past news, news about being awarded new contracts is very common. On the other hand, a mature business like BWXT experiences slow business growth, which is a critical factor for potential investors to consider.

Since its core business depends on government needs, BWXT has strived to expand its commercial operations in recent years. As of the last quarter end, the commercial operations only account for 20% of revenue. While this part is expected to achieve a higher growth rate in relative to core business, we may not overly emphasize its significance for now.

How To Come Up With A Reasonable Estimate Of Future Growth Rate?

I can expect some disagreement regarding the growth forecast, but luckily, we can segment BWXT's realized growth with its Q2 earnings report.

Government Operations

The company documented an overall revenue growth of 11.3% in the first half of the year 2024. In the latest quarter, the government operations still account for 80% of revenue.

This growth rate seems an earnings surprise to the market, and thus led to positive returns. But the question is whether a growth rate of ~10% is sustainable for the government operation business. To me, it is unrealistic. As I discussed earlier, the growth of this part is highly correlated with nuclear needs, and the following picture, for example, shows that the BWXT management believes a sustainable growth rate should be ~3-5% with a breakdown of future nuclear needs:

Therefore, when making future forecasts, it's more reasonable to assume a growth rate of 3-5% for the government operations business. I would like to mention that the revenue growth rate has its fluctuation, so it's important not to weigh the most recent value too much.

Commercial Operations

In the latest disclosure, BWXT aggregated BWXT commercial nuclear and BWXT medical into BWXT commercial, so we cannot track the growth of each business. Overall, commercial operations achieved 17% in the first two quarters, suggesting a reasonably high growth rate. Over time, the revenue and operating profits of commercial operations grew at a medium speed. So how do we estimate this part for the upcoming years?

BWXT's management has reiterated a bunch on the prospects of commercial operations, and BWXT has incurred significant capital expenditure in recent years. As a result, I suspect the optimism about future growth greatly contributed to current valuation. I appreciate their efforts to expand their business into areas where they can leverage their expertise. The close relationship between the commercial business and core business should allow BWXT to have a good knowledge of many aspects of the new business, such as production, cost, potential market and lifecycle management. I would say this is a good example of corporate diversification, which we all should applaud.

I would like to point out that the growth opportunities also come with risks. Over the past three years, the new nuclear drugs have gained a lot of spotlight. There is no doubt that BWXT has made significant efforts to develop its medicine business. For example, BWXT Medical submitted a drug master file for Actinium-225 API to U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) this June. Before that, BWXT Medical submitted an application of Tc-99m in September 2022. This is where risks came in. The management expected FDA approval last year, but it has been delayed for a year and has not yet been approved as of today. While I admire the management's ambition of becoming leader in the new market, this kind of risk may hinder actual performance.

Given that, we should still expect strong performance from the BWXT commercial segment in the upcoming years, but there is indeed some uncertainty about the growth rate of commercial operations. In a bullish case, let's say a growth rate of 30% for the next 5 years. And since the commercial segment is growing relatively faster than the core business. Let's assume a higher average weight of 30%. Then we can come to a bullish revenue estimate for the next few years, which is 11.8% (4% * 70% + 30% *30%). We can also calculate a bearish case where we assume a growth rate of 10% for the commercial segment. Then, this should give us 5.8% (4% * 70% + 10% *30%).

How is BWXT's valuation compared to peers?

Before factoring in other valuation elements, I would like to use an easy ratio to begin with - PEG (PE/Growth rate). If this rate is high, it means that the growth prospects may have already been priced in. Otherwise, this may suggest a good investment opportunity for investors. With the previous estimates, PEG ratio will give us a ~3.5 in a bullish case, and ~6 in a bearish case. This ratio itself is relatively high. But it seems reasonable compared to peer companies within the same or similar industries. Again, unless we believe that the industry will be valued more in the future, there is no reason to believe that the stock price of BWXT will go up due to undervaluation.

Then, if we look at the P/E ratio, which does not take into much account the growth rate, we may become more worried about overvaluation - within the industry, BWXT seems to have the highest P/E ratio.

Should we factor in predictability?

We may easily compute an average growth rate for the next 5 years. But one should also consider the predictability of growth rate, which affects stock volatility and analyst ratings. So, should people factor in BWXT's predictability or financial stability?

While in general, people should value companies with higher predictability more because they come with less uncertainty, I suspect if this is not the case for BWXT. If we look at the peer companies of BWXT, most of them have government or government-like businesses. Despite some fluctuations in government budgetary allocations, the revenue generated from government contracts is highly sustainable and reliable for now. So, within the industry, BWXT is not an exception.

Conclusion

Overall, these factors, combined with the current premium valuation, suggest that BWXT has little room for stock going up at this time. I would highly recommend BWXT again when its price becomes more tempting again. At the same time, I suggest potential buyers pay close attention to the company's news because FDA approval should be around the corner. For the current shareholders and potential investors, it is advisable to hold the stock to avoid possible overvaluation losses and seek opportunities with more favorable risk-reward profiles.

data.title: How Deep And Wide Is Nvidia's Moat?
data.description:
Thesis

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) has come a long way since its founding in a Denny’s diner. Now the company’s at the forefront of Generative AI (“AI”). On 5/23, Nvidia had its iPhone Moment when its 1Q’24 (see “Data Center” in chart) results squashed revenue & earnings estimates. It issued 2Q’24 revenue guidance of $11Bn, vs. just $7Bn analysts were expecting. Nvidia found that they could repurpose their gaming GPU towards AI, and boy did they! This article tries to determine if Nvidia’s share price momentum can continue by determining if it has a wide moat. If it does, then Nvidia’s a secular growth story like Cisco (CSCO) of the 1990s, else Nvidia may just be reminiscent of Zoom’s (ZM) 2020 ephemeral experience.

Introduction

Nvidia’s a name everyone knows, I’ve known Nvidia since its IPO, AI is an acronym that everyone knows. What we don’t know is how sustainable are Nvidia’s growth and profitability. Nvidia must have a deep & wide moat in order to maintain its high P/E ratio (and valuation). Hence, this article will focus solely on Nvidia’s positioning (see market share pie chart) within the semiconductor industry.

The growing TAM

The benefits of AI can clearly be seen by anyone that has ever used Gemini, ChatGPT or Perplexity. But the bigger TAM will be corporate enterprises. According to a study by Morgan Stanley, there’s about $38 trillion in costs that could be reduced by corporate adoption of AI.

The overall AI chip market size in 2024 ranges from $30T to $123T, depending on how the market is defined (the broadest TAM is included in the chart below). The CAGR is 20.4%, but I’ve seen projections as high as 30%.

Presently, most companies utilizing AI are using some form of Gen-AI Copilot, such as Salesforce’s (CRM) Einstein. Atlassian (TEAM) and GitLab (GTLB) are using AI to speed-up developers’ programming. Given that Copilot is the first successful use-case and a potentially high return on investment, I expect the TAM to be larger and last longer than first estimated a year ago.

Industry Structure

The industry, as well as its share prices, are highly volatile and subject to boom-bust periods. Each decade there is a segment that is hot such as CPU (“Intel inside”) DRAM memory, Flash and Graphics Cards. Today’s shiny object is AI. However, I would argue that it’s not a “flash in the pan” given that Nvidia’s business position is quite strong. Some of these supporting factors (based on Michael Porter’s framework) are: high Entry Barriers, weak Bargaining Power of suppliers, Low-to-moderate Bargaining power of buyers (high demand, double-ordering) and Low-to-moderate threat from Substitute Products. Focusing in on Entry barriers (the key determinate of earnings sustainability in my view) it is characterized as the following:

  • High Capital Requirements and Economies of Scale: New competitors would require significant capital, Intellectual Property related to GPU design, and a build-out could take years.
  • Technological Expertise: Nvidia’s domain experience is huge, not just in chips, but in the surrounding Stack.
  • High Switching Costs: Heavy use of software and partnerships (Dell, HP, Accenture, Deloitte, KPMG…) make it very difficult for customers to leave the Nvidia ecosystem. Nvidia’s reputation for making high-performing GPUs contributes to customer loyalty.
  • Ability to Execute: Nvidia’s continued to introduce new versions of chips faster than is typical for the semiconductor industry.

For example, let’s look at two of Nvidia’s software offerings. The first one, CUDA, is a parallel processing platform that allows developers to use GPUs for general purpose processing. It supports multiple languages and includes a toolkit that provides libraries, debugging tools etc. Nvidia also developed a software stack for data centers that helps companies quickly assemble GPUs, communications (i.e., networking) and related supplies so that everything works well together. I like to think of Nvidia’s solution akin to building a modular house that can be built quickly and everything works seamlessly together.

Recent Financial Performance

On 8/24, Nvidia reported yet another earnings and revenue beat and raised guidance. However, shares declined sharply on delays to the next gen GPU (“Blackwell”) and gross margin headwinds. The FY’25 GPM guide is about 75% (just 50bps below consensus estimates) but this is down from a 78% quarterly peak. Other detractors were U.S. restrictions on the Chinese market, shortages of data-center space and electric power.

The declining rate of revenue growth (which is normal for a company of this scale) may have worried investors (see table). Given the huge TAM and CEO Jensen Huang’s recent comments that demand is “insane” alleviates any growth worries in my view.

Conventional Valuation measures for Nvidia

At first glance, Nvidia’s valuation is extremely high, with SA assigning a "D-" rating and my “Adjusted Overvaluation” is 53%. (The Adjustment excludes duplicate metrics.). Note that the PEG ratio (1.28) is below the sector median (1.93). This is astonishing when one realizes that most chip companies are selling commodities in a boom-bust industry – this usually translates to low PEG ratios for the sector.

Price/Earnings Ratio of Nvidia (High Moat vs. No Moat)

By using Nvidia’s forward revenue estimates, I was able to calculate forward earnings/share and forward P/E ratios. Shares outstanding were lowered slightly every year. The tables below show Nvidia’s revenue growth and its P/E ratio (for High Moat and No Moat). The “High Moat” ratios maintain Nvidia’s TTM profit margin, while the “No Moat” ratios are based on a five-year average of profit margins prior to the company’s FY’24 iPhone moment.

As you can see, there is a huge difference in the two ratios, as the No Moat ratio is sharply higher due to earnings compression (so the denominator is getting reduced). What is not shown in the chart is that in a No Moat environment, Nvidia would simply be another commodity player among many. Consequently, its P/E premium would no longer exist, and we would need to benchmark the P/E ratio to a much lower level. It’s worth repeating that we have two moving parts in a “No Moat” environment: 1) earnings would compress and 2) the P/E benchmark (about 20x) would be reduced to reflect higher earnings volatility and a commodity end-market.

Risks to my bullish thesis

It’s possible that Nvidia’s huge profit margins make it a target for both competitors and customers, as well as the involvement of venture capitalists. In fact, VCs are pouring money into the space and the Magnificent 7 are working on their in-house solutions. Competitors, ranging from sleepy stalwarts like Intel to a reinvigorated AMD, are aggressively introducing AI chips. What’s more, the industry’s “usual-suspects” have formed an open-source consortium (“UXL Foundation”) to compete with Nvidia’s closed CUDA software system. My view is that collectively these efforts will “chip away” at Nvidia’s dominance over time with little threat of replacing Nvidia.

Another risk is Nvidia’s dependence on the Magnificent 7 (and China) so customer diversity is very low. The flip-side is that Nvidia isn’t dependent on risky internet startups, as was the case for Cisco Systems in the 1990s.

Conclusion

Long-term, I expect AI native SaaS companies to be the largest beneficiaries of the AI revolution. However, Nvidia’s clearly the best situated company in the space right now. Given Nvidia’s high moat, I believe it will easily meet analysts’ revenue growth targets, as well as maintain high profit margins. Hence, Nvidia’s shares seem underpriced considering that its forward P/E ratio is below 50x in spite of 108% projected revenue growth and 138% EPS growth. Nvidia remains a “Buy.”
